---
title: "File formats"
subtitle: "CSCI 4623 — Digital Forensics"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Tasks"
    number-sections: false
    code-copy: true
    theme: cosmo
    css: tsk-styles.css
---

## Overview

This walkthrough accompanies the File Formats lecture module.
You will examine a curated set of fabricated sample files using the same tools
an examiner would reach for during triage and analysis: `file`, `exiftool`,
`xxd`, `pngcheck`, `binwalk`, `unzip`, and a set of Python helper scripts.

Each task targets a specific format family or forensic technique.
The sample files contain planted artifacts — metadata fields, hidden chunks,
embedded archives, and timestamp inconsistencies — designed to surface the
concepts from lecture in a controlled setting.

**Estimated time:** 60–90 minutes

**Prerequisites:** Run `setup_formats_lab.sh` before beginning.

---

## Task 0 — Verify setup and run first-pass identification

### Motivation

Before examining any individual file, an examiner surveys the entire file set
using automated identification tools. This establishes a baseline and immediately
flags any format anomalies.

### 0.1 Verify the lab directory

```bash
ls -lh ~/formats-lab/
```

You should see 15 files. If any are missing, re-run `setup_formats_lab.sh`.

### 0.2 Run `file` across all files

```bash
cd ~/formats-lab
file -i *
```

Expected output (order may vary):

```
archive.zip:    application/zip; charset=binary
audio.mp3:      audio/mpeg; charset=binary
document.pdf:   application/pdf; charset=binary
firmware.bin:   image/jpeg; charset=binary
graphic.png:    image/png; charset=binary
legacy.doc:     application/msword; charset=binary
mismatch1.pdf:  image/jpeg; charset=binary
mismatch2.docx: application/zip; charset=binary
normal.elf:     application/x-executable; charset=binary
normal.exe:     application/x-dosexec; charset=binary
packed.elf:     application/x-executable; charset=binary
packed.exe:     application/x-dosexec; charset=binary
photo.jpg:      image/jpeg; charset=binary
report.docx:    application/zip; charset=binary
video.mp4:      video/mp4; charset=binary
```

### 0.3 Identify the extension mismatches

Examine the output above. Two files have extensions that contradict their
detected MIME type:

- `mismatch1.pdf` — reported as `image/jpeg`, not `application/pdf`
- `mismatch2.docx` — reported as `application/zip` (ZIP, not OOXML)

Note that `report.docx` is *also* reported as `application/zip` — this is
correct, because OOXML files are ZIP archives. The distinction is that
`mismatch2.docx` is a *raw* ZIP with no OOXML internal structure.

Confirm with:

```bash
unzip -l mismatch2.docx | head -5
unzip -l report.docx | head -5
```

The `report.docx` listing will show `word/`, `docProps/`, and `_rels/`
directories characteristic of OOXML. The `mismatch2.docx` listing will show
the raw `data/` and `readme.txt` entries from a plain ZIP archive.

### 0.4 Run TrID on the mismatched files

```bash
trid mismatch1.pdf
trid mismatch2.docx
```

TrID returns a ranked list of candidate formats with confidence percentages.
Record the top result for each file and compare with what `file` reported.

---

## Task 1 — JPEG dissection

### Motivation

JPEG files carry more forensic information than their compressed pixel data
suggests. The EXIF APP1 segment embeds device identity, capture timestamps,
GPS coordinates, and an independent thumbnail that may preserve a pre-edit
version of the image.

### 1.1 Confirm the JPEG structure with `xxd`

```bash
xxd photo.jpg | head -4
```

Expected output (first two bytes are the JPEG SOI marker; the next two are the APP1 marker):

```
00000000: ffd8 ffe1 xxxx 4578 6966 0000 ...     ......Exif..
```

- `FF D8` — Start of Image (SOI) — 2-byte marker, no length field
- `FF E1` — APP1 marker (EXIF segment follows); the `xx xx` after it is the segment length

Confirm the End of Image marker at the file's tail:

```bash
xxd photo.jpg | tail -2
```

The last two bytes should be `FF D9` (EOI marker).

### 1.2 Extract all EXIF metadata with `exiftool`

```bash
exiftool photo.jpg
```

Locate and record:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `Make` | |
| `Camera Model Name` | |
| `Software` | |
| `Date/Time Original` | |
| `GPS Latitude` | |
| `GPS Longitude` | |
| `GPS Altitude` | |

Note the `Software` field — it identifies `Adobe Photoshop`, while
`Date/Time Original` reflects the original capture time (`2024:03:15`).
The `DateTime` field (the last-modified-by-software timestamp) is later
(`2024:04:02`) — 18 days after capture — confirming the file was
post-processed after the original shoot.

### 1.3 Extract the embedded thumbnail

```bash
exiftool -ThumbnailImage -b photo.jpg > thumbnail.jpg
file -i thumbnail.jpg
```

The `-b` flag suppresses the tag name, writing only the raw binary data.
Confirm the output is a valid JPEG.

### 1.4 Compare thumbnail with main image

```bash
exiftool -ImageSize photo.jpg
exiftool -ThumbnailImageSize photo.jpg 2>/dev/null || exiftool -ImageSize thumbnail.jpg
```

Open both files in an image viewer or use `identify` (ImageMagick) if available:

```bash
identify photo.jpg thumbnail.jpg 2>/dev/null
```

The thumbnail is a different color from the main image — it was generated
from the original pre-edit version and not updated when the main image was
modified. This thumbnail-vs-main discrepancy is a forensic indicator of
post-capture manipulation.

### 1.5 Use the helper script

```bash
python3 ~/formats-lab/scripts/inspect_jpeg.py photo.jpg
```

The script confirms the SOI marker, prints the IFD0, Exif, and GPS IFDs,
and extracts the thumbnail to `photo_thumbnail.jpg`.

---

## Task 2 — PNG chunk analysis

### Motivation

PNG uses a chunked structure where ancillary chunks carry arbitrary metadata.
These chunks are invisible to image viewers but trivially readable with the
right tools. They can carry planted metadata, operator notes, or hidden data.

### 2.1 Identify all chunks with `pngcheck`

```bash
pngcheck -v graphic.png
```

`pngcheck` validates CRCs and lists every chunk with its offset and length.
Look for any chunk types beyond `IHDR`, `IDAT`, and `IEND`.

You should see at least one `iTXt` chunk and one `tEXt` chunk in the listing.

### 2.2 Run the helper script

```bash
python3 ~/formats-lab/scripts/inspect_png.py graphic.png
```

Expected output includes a chunk inventory table followed by the decoded
text chunk contents. Record:

- The `iTXt` keyword and text value
- The `tEXt` keyword and text value

The `iTXt` chunk contains operator metadata planted during fabrication:
case number, operator email, and tool name. In a real investigation, this
content would be recorded as evidence and its source investigated.

### 2.3 Examine the raw bytes

Locate the `iTXt` marker directly:

```bash
xxd graphic.png | grep -A2 "6954 5874"
```

`69 54 58 74` is the ASCII encoding of `iTXt`.
The four bytes before the type field are the chunk length (big-endian).

---

## Task 3 — PDF forensics

### Motivation

PDF files embed document metadata that persists across format conversions and
printing. Incremental updates allow a PDF to be edited without replacing the
original content, creating a layered edit history visible at the byte level.

### 3.1 Extract metadata with `exiftool`

```bash
exiftool document.pdf
```

Record the following fields:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `Author` | |
| `Creator` | |
| `Producer` | |
| `Create Date` | |
| `Modify Date` | |

Note that `Creator` identifies the application that produced the original
document (`Microsoft Word`), while `Producer` identifies the conversion
tool (`Acrobat Distiller`). These are separate provenance indicators.

### 3.2 Identify the incremental update

```bash
grep -c "%%EOF" document.pdf
```

A value of `2` confirms two `%%EOF` markers — the original file and one
incremental update appended afterward.

Locate the `startxref` values:

```bash
grep -n "startxref" document.pdf
```

Two `startxref` entries will appear. The second (later) one belongs to the
incremental update. The trailer of the update contains a `/Prev` key pointing
back to the original xref table offset.

### 3.3 Inspect the update object

```bash
grep -A4 "Incremental update" document.pdf
```

The appended annotation object contains the reviewer's name and timestamp —
information added after the document was originally authored.
Both the original author (`Margaret Chen`) and the reviewer (`D. Torres`)
are now recoverable from the file.

### 3.4 Scan for active content indicators

`pdfid.py` was staged to `~/formats-lab/pdftools/` by the setup script:

```bash
python3 ~/formats-lab/pdftools/pdfid.py document.pdf
```

`pdfid.py` counts occurrences of active-content keywords. For `document.pdf`
the counts for `/JS`, `/JavaScript`, `/Launch`, and `/OpenAction` should all
be zero — this is a benign document.

---

## Task 4 — Office document metadata

### Motivation

Both legacy OLE-based Office formats (`.doc`) and modern OOXML formats
(`.docx`) embed rich author and revision metadata that survives format
conversion. OOXML's ZIP container allows direct inspection of its XML
parts without specialized tools.

### 4.1 OOXML: inspect with `exiftool`

```bash
exiftool report.docx
```

Record:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `Creator` | |
| `Last Modified By` | |
| `Create Date` | |
| `Modify Date` | |
| `Revision Number` | |

### 4.2 OOXML: inspect the ZIP structure

```bash
unzip -l report.docx
```

Identify the `docProps/core.xml` and `docProps/app.xml` parts.

### 4.3 Read core.xml directly

```bash
unzip -p report.docx docProps/core.xml | xmllint --format -
```

The `dc:creator` field holds the original author. The `cp:lastModifiedBy`
field holds the account that last saved the file. These may differ — here
they identify two separate individuals (`James Wilson` and `Sarah Okonkwo`),
establishing an edit chain.

### 4.4 Read app.xml

```bash
unzip -p report.docx docProps/app.xml | xmllint --format -
```

`app.xml` exposes the application name and version used to save the file,
plus the company name if set in the authoring application's profile.

### 4.5 OLE: inspect legacy.doc with the helper script

```bash
python3 ~/formats-lab/scripts/inspect_ole.py legacy.doc
```

The script lists all streams in the OLE directory tree, then dumps the
`SummaryInformation` property set. Compare the `Author` and `Last saved by`
fields with those extracted from `report.docx` in step 4.1 — they derive
from the same source document and should match.

Note that the `App name` field will show `LibreOffice` rather than
`Microsoft Word`. This is expected: the `.doc` file was produced by
converting `report.docx` with LibreOffice, so the conversion tool's
name appears in the OLE metadata rather than the original authoring
application. In a real investigation, an `App name` value that does
not match the stated document provenance is worth noting.

---

## Task 5 — Archive forensics

### Motivation

ZIP archives expose metadata even when their contents are encrypted or
inaccessible. The EOCD comment field, per-entry timestamps, and the
central directory structure are all available without decryption and
can carry intelligence about the archive's origin and handling.

### 5.1 List archive contents with `zipinfo`

```bash
zipinfo -v archive.zip
```

`zipinfo -v` (verbose) shows per-entry compression method, timestamps,
CRC-32 values, and the EOCD comment field if present.

Locate the `Archive comment` section at the bottom of the output.
Record the comment text — it contains a case number, collection date,
and examiner name.

### 5.2 Examine timestamps

From the `zipinfo -v` output, note the timestamp fields per entry.
Each entry carries two timestamp representations:

- **MS-DOS timestamp** (2-second resolution) — in the local file header
  and central directory; always present
- **Unix extended timestamp (`UT` extra field)** — 32-bit Unix epoch value
  in both the local header (mtime + atime) and central directory (mtime only);
  provides full Unix-precision timing and identifies the creating platform

Compare the staggered mtime values across entries — they differ by 17 seconds
each, reflecting the order in which files were added to the archive.

### 5.3 Scan firmware.bin with `binwalk`

```bash
binwalk --signature firmware.bin
```

Expected output:

```
DECIMAL       HEXADECIMAL     DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
0             0x0             JPEG image data, JFIF standard 1.01
<offset>      <hex>           Zip archive data, at least v2.0 to extract,
                              name: config.txt
<offset>      <hex>           Zip archive data, at least v2.0 to extract,
                              name: keys/device.key
<offset>      <hex>           End of Zip archive, footer length: 22
```

`binwalk` identified both the JPEG at offset 0 and a ZIP archive appended
after the JPEG's EOI marker. The file is valid as a JPEG — any image viewer
will display it correctly — but the appended ZIP data is invisible to those tools.

### 5.4 Extract the embedded archive

```bash
binwalk --extract firmware.bin
ls _firmware.bin.extracted/
```

`binwalk` creates an extraction directory. Examine the recovered files:

```bash
cat _firmware.bin.extracted/*/config.txt
```

The config file contains a password hash and device credentials — content
that would be invisible to a cursory file inspection.

### 5.5 Entropy plot

```bash
binwalk --entropy firmware.bin
```

The entropy plot will show a relatively flat region (the JPEG compressed data)
followed by a second region corresponding to the appended ZIP. Both regions
have similar entropy profiles since both use deflate compression.

---

## Task 6 — ELF binary forensics

### Motivation

ELF binaries carry identification artifacts that survive stripping and
can attribute a binary to a specific compiler version, build environment,
and even a specific project path. Entropy analysis exposes packing or
encryption that defeats static string extraction.

### 6.1 Basic identification

```bash
file normal.elf
file packed.elf
```

Both files will be identified as ELF executables. Note whether `file`
reports each as `stripped` or `not stripped`.

### 6.2 Section inventory with `readelf`

```bash
readelf -S --wide normal.elf | head -30
readelf -S --wide packed.elf | head -30
```

Compare the section tables. `normal.elf` will have many sections
including `.symtab`, `.strtab`, and `.debug_*` sections.
`packed.elf` will have a minimal section table — a hallmark of packed binaries.

### 6.3 Build ID and notes

```bash
readelf -n normal.elf
```

The `GNU` note section contains the Build ID — a SHA-1 or MD5 hash of
the binary's content at build time. This identifier is stable across
identical builds and can link multiple samples to the same build.

### 6.4 Compiler version from .comment

```bash
readelf -p .comment normal.elf
```

The `.comment` section contains a null-terminated string naming the compiler
and version used to produce the binary. This narrows the build environment
to a specific GCC release and distribution.

Try the same on `packed.elf`:

```bash
readelf -p .comment packed.elf 2>&1
```

A packed binary typically has no `.comment` section — the packer replaces
the original section table with its own stub.

### 6.5 Entropy analysis

```bash
binwalk -E normal.elf
binwalk -E packed.elf
```

`normal.elf` will show varying entropy: lower in `.rodata` and data sections,
moderate in `.text` (compiled code). `packed.elf` will show uniformly high
entropy (approaching 8.0 bits/byte) across its primary load segment — the
signature of compressed or encrypted payload.

### 6.6 Helper script

```bash
python3 ~/formats-lab/scripts/inspect_elf.py normal.elf
```

The script prints the ELF identification fields, section inventory,
Build ID, and `.comment` content in a single pass.

---

## Task 7 — PE binary forensics

### Motivation

PE files embed compilation metadata, version resources, and import tables
that fingerprint the build environment and reveal capability. The compile
timestamp, PDB path, and Rich header are artifacts that persist even when
other metadata is stripped.

### 7.1 Basic identification

```bash
file normal.exe
file packed.exe
```

Both should be identified as PE32+ (or PE32) executables.

### 7.2 Section inventory and entropy

```bash
binwalk -E normal.exe
binwalk -E packed.exe
```

`normal.exe` will show characteristic section entropy: moderate for `.text`,
lower for `.rdata` and `.data`. `packed.exe` will show a single
high-entropy segment — the compressed original binary.

### 7.3 Inspect the PE with the helper script

```bash
python3 ~/formats-lab/scripts/inspect_pe.py normal.exe
```

Record:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Machine type | |
| Compile timestamp | |
| Entry point RVA | |
| Sections | |
| Imports | |

### 7.4 Compare import tables

```bash
python3 ~/formats-lab/scripts/inspect_pe.py normal.exe 2>&1 | grep -A20 "Imports"
python3 ~/formats-lab/scripts/inspect_pe.py packed.exe 2>&1 | grep -A20 "Imports"
```

`normal.exe` will list its imported DLLs and functions (`user32.dll`,
`kernel32.dll`, etc.). `packed.exe` will show a minimal import table —
often just the loader stub's imports (`LoadLibrary`, `GetProcAddress`) —
since the real imports are resolved at runtime by the unpacker.

---

## Task 8 — MP4 and MP3 metadata

### Motivation

Video and audio files from mobile devices embed GPS coordinates, device
identity, and encoder information in their container metadata. This data
survives transcoding poorly — comparing encoder strings and timestamps
can reveal whether a file is an original recording or a re-encoded copy.

### 8.1 MP4: full metadata dump

```bash
exiftool video.mp4
```

Locate and record:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `Create Date` | |
| `Modify Date` | |
| `GPS Latitude` | |
| `GPS Longitude` | |
| `GPS Altitude` | |
| `Encoder` / `Author` | |

Note the `Create Date` format — MP4 stores timestamps as seconds since
1904-01-01 (the Mac epoch); `exiftool` converts this to a readable date.

### 8.2 Compare MP4 timestamps with file system timestamps

```bash
stat video.mp4
exiftool -CreateDate -ModifyDate video.mp4
```

The file system `mtime` reflects when the file was last written on this
system (during lab setup). The `CreateDate` embedded in the `mvhd` atom
reflects the metadata injected at creation time. In a real investigation,
a discrepancy between these timestamps indicates the file was copied or
transferred from another system.

### 8.3 MP3: ID3 tag dump

```bash
exiftool audio.mp3
```

Locate:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `Title` | |
| `Artist` | |
| `Album` | |
| `Year` | |
| `Comment` | |
| `Picture MIME Type` | |
| `Picture Type` | |

Note that `exiftool` reports the presence of an embedded picture (`APIC` frame)
with a MIME type of `image/jpeg`. This is an independent JPEG artifact embedded
inside the MP3 file.

### 8.4 Extract the embedded APIC image

```bash
exiftool -Picture -b audio.mp3 > embedded_cover.jpg
file -i embedded_cover.jpg
exiftool embedded_cover.jpg
```

Confirm the extracted file is a valid JPEG. In an investigation, this
embedded image would be examined as an independent artifact — it may carry
its own EXIF metadata, GPS data, or content of evidentiary value.

### 8.5 Verify the URL frame

```bash
exiftool -UserDefinedURL audio.mp3 2>/dev/null || \
    python3 -c "
from mutagen.id3 import ID3
tags = ID3('audio.mp3')
for key in tags:
    if key.startswith('W'):
        print(key, ':', tags[key])
"
```

The `WXXX` frame contains a URL reference. In real-world evidence, URL frames
have been used to link files to web resources, accounts, or distribution platforms.

---

## Summary

Work through the table below to consolidate the artifacts recovered across all tasks.

| File | Format | Key artifact recovered |
|---|---|---|
| `photo.jpg` | JPEG | GPS coordinates; thumbnail-main mismatch |
| `graphic.png` | PNG | Hidden `iTXt` chunk: operator, case number |
| `document.pdf` | PDF | Author chain; incremental update with reviewer |
| `report.docx` | OOXML | Creator / last-modified-by chain; revision 7 |
| `legacy.doc` | OLE/CFB | SummaryInformation author fields |
| `archive.zip` | ZIP | EOCD comment: case number, examiner, date |
| `firmware.bin` | JPEG+ZIP | Appended ZIP with config file and key |
| `normal.elf` | ELF | Build ID; compiler version in `.comment` |
| `packed.elf` | ELF (packed) | High entropy; stripped section table |
| `normal.exe` | PE | Compile timestamp; imports; version resource |
| `packed.exe` | PE (packed) | Minimal imports; high-entropy `.text` |
| `video.mp4` | MP4 | GPS atom; encoder; Mac epoch timestamps |
| `audio.mp3` | MP3 | ID3v2 tags; embedded JPEG (APIC); URL frame |
| `mismatch1.pdf` | JPEG (renamed) | Extension contradiction detected by `file` |
| `mismatch2.docx` | ZIP (renamed) | Extension contradiction; raw ZIP, not OOXML |

### Questions for review

1. `document.pdf` has two `%%EOF` markers. What does each one delimit,
   and what forensic significance does the structure have?

2. The `Software` field in `photo.jpg` names Adobe Photoshop, while
   `DateTimeOriginal` predates any plausible editing session.
   What two conclusions can you draw, and what additional evidence
   would strengthen each?

3. `packed.elf` shows near-uniform high entropy across its primary segment.
   Does this confirm the binary is malicious? What additional steps would
   you take before drawing that conclusion?

4. `firmware.bin` is identified as `image/jpeg` by both `file` and most
   image viewers. Describe the mechanism that allows the appended ZIP to
   coexist with the valid JPEG structure without corrupting it.

5. The `archive.zip` EOCD comment field contains 89 bytes of text.
   What is the maximum length of this field, and what constraint
   governs that limit?
